questions.prompt:
Hannah, a first grade student, got out of her classroom seat many times without permission. Her teacher raised her eyebrows before taking Hannah to the principal’s office. The next day another student, Sarah, started to get out of her seat without permission. As soon as the teacher raised her eyebrows, Sarah quickly returned to her seat and the rest of the class sat quietly.

Which of the following concepts best applies to the behavior of the class?

questions.prompt: Craig walks from outside on a bright sunny day into a dark movie theater. Which of the following types of cells are initially responsible for his adaptation to the darker space?

options.content: Glial cells, which are responsible for the process of light adaptation
Ganglion cells, which are responsible for processing visual information and transmitting it to the brain
Rods, which are responsible for adaptation to different light levels
Interneurons, which are responsible for processing perceptual information in the brain

questions.prompt:
Justina believed that she would never become addicted to smoking cigarettes because her parents taught her that it was wrong. Six months ago, she started smoking with her friends on weekends. Now she smokes throughout the week and believes that smoking is no worse than drinking coffee.

Which of the following factors would best explain Justina’s current belief about smoking?

options.content: Her highly authoritative parents likely contributed to her current belief.
Her growth mindset has led to a change in her beliefs about smoking.
Cognitive dissonance led to her current belief.
The sunk-cost fallacy influenced her current belief.

questions.prompt:
Jenna used treats to train a pig to put a wooden coin into a toy machine. She began by giving a treat whenever the pig got close to the coin. Then, she only gave the pig a treat when it touched the coin with its snout. After that, she only gave the pig a treat when it picked up the coin with its teeth. Eventually, Jenna only gave the pig a treat when it picked up the coin and put it into the toy machine.

Which of the following approaches did Jenna use?

Keenan’s thinking is most clearly an example of which of the following concepts?

questions.prompt:
When Rowan was six years old, his father took him to see a tennis match. Rowan enjoyed learning the rules of the game and watching the players hit the ball back and forth across the net with their tennis racquets.

Five months later, Rowan’s aunt took him to a park to play pickleball, a game similar to tennis. He had a great afternoon learning to hit the ball back and forth with the pickleball paddle instead of a tennis racquet. That night, Rowan told his parents that he had a great time playing “tennis” with his aunt.

Which of the following concepts relates to Rowan’s schema in this scenario?

questions.prompt: Researchers examined infants’ depth perception in what is known as the visual cliff experiment. Which of the following statements might be an ethical concern related to using a visual cliff with infants?

options.content: Infants could have experienced distress when they looked at the visual drop or were encouraged by their mothers to cross it.
The researchers did not obtain informed consent from the infants participating in the study.
Infants’ depth perception could have been fundamentally altered by the study, putting the children at risk.
It is always unethical to study how children develop fears, such as a fear of heights.

questions.prompt:
Researchers randomly selected 300 adults whom they interviewed about their perception of crime rates from 2002 to 2016. These perceived crime rates were then compared to actual crime rates, and the results of this comparison over that period are depicted in the graph.

Changes in Actual Crime Versus Perceived Crime

  • All values are approximate.
  • The following 2 lines are shown:
    • Real crime
    • Perceived crime
  • The Real crime line:
    • Begins at 2002: negative 6.5%
    • Falls to 2004: negative 10.8%
    • Rises to 2008: negative 5%
    • Remains level to 2010: negative 5%
    • Rises to 2012: negative 0.1%
    • Falls to 2014: negative 8.3%
    • Rises to 2016: negative 2.3%
  • The Perceived crime line:
    • Begins at 2002: negative 0.7%
    • Rises to 2004: 0%
    • Rises to 2006: 1.9%
    • Falls to 2008: 1%
    • Rises to 2010: 2.1%
    • Falls to 2012: negative 2.8%
    • Rises to 2014: negative 0.7%
    • Rises to 2016: 4%

Which of the following conclusions is correct based on the overall results of this study?

options.content: Participants expected the actual crime rate to remain relatively stable over the length of the study.
Participants mostly perceived the crime rate to be higher than it was.
Participants’ predictions accurately tracked the actual crime rate.
Participants believed that the crime rate decreased slowly over the length of the study.

questions.explanation: Answer key not available yet for this imported exam.

Institutional Review Boards will not approve research that studies conformity.

A property owner refuses to rent to families with children, individuals with disabilities, and individuals over the age of 65.

The number of siblings a person has

questions.prompt:
Dr. Xiang examined a 21-year-old patient who had fallen and hit their head. After a brain scan was conducted, Dr. Xiang discovered that the patient was missing their cerebellum. Dr. Xiang extensively interviewed the patient and their family and learned that the patient had a long history of balance and coordination problems.

Based on this patient, Dr. Xiang tells her colleague that anyone who is missing their cerebellum will always experience balance and coordination problems.

Which of the following responses from Dr. Xiang’s colleague would be the most appropriate?

options.content: Dr. Xiang cannot generalize her findings to the larger population because she used a single case study.
Dr. Xiang cannot make causal inferences because she conducted a naturalistic observation.
Dr. Xiang can generalize her findings to the larger population because she had a representative sample.
Dr. Xiang can make causal inferences because she conducted a longitudinal study.

questions.prompt:
Wyatt can hold a bottle between his fingers and palm at age six months, yet he is unable to grasp a piece of cereal between his thumb and index finger until age nine months.

Which of the following descriptions best represents the developmental perspective regarding these milestones in physical development?

options.content: The age at which each grasping technique develops is dependent on the frequency of practice and modeling.
The exact age at which each grasping technique develops varies from infant to infant, but the order in which these physical stages occur is the same.
Most infants learn the finger-to-palm grasp more quickly than the thumb-to-index-finger grasp because of early bottle feeding.
Grasping an object between the index finger and thumb requires a level of adrenaline in the prefrontal cortex not present at age six months.

questions.prompt: Which of the following scenarios is an example of the gambler’s fallacy?

options.content: Anita’s friend refused her request to donate $200 to a charity, but then her friend agreed to donate $20 to the charity.
Marie was so focused on picking a card from the cards laid out on a table that she did not notice when the magician exchanged places with his assistant.
After his favorite team lost three games in a row, Doug was certain that they would win the next one, so he bought an expensive ticket to attend the next game.
After spending over two hours negotiating at the car dealership, Sean decided that he should purchase a car, even though he was not getting his target purchase price.

Ken had a very challenging interaction with one of his closest friends in which hurtful words were exchanged. After considering possible solutions, Ken decided that while it might be uncomfortable at first, the best solution was to confront his friend directly to discuss what happened and work toward a solution.

Which of the following approaches to alleviating a stressful situation is most consistent with Ken’s strategy?

options.content: An external locus of control

questions.prompt:
Researchers at an advertising agency conducted a study to determine whether classical conditioning principles could influence consumers' preferences for a particular product. They randomly assigned 100 college students from a nearby university to one of two groups.

Both groups watched a commercial for the same brand of shoes. One group watched a commercial that did not feature a celebrity endorsement, while the second group watched a commercial that featured a celebrity endorsement from a physically attractive celebrity. The participants were exposed to these ads multiple times a day for two weeks.

At the end of the two weeks, the participants were asked to rate, on a scale of 1 to 10, how likely they were to purchase the brand of shoes advertised in the commercial they watched.

Which of the following statements most accurately describes why this study is an experiment?

options.content: The researchers operationally defined the variables.
The participants were randomly assigned to one of two groups.
The sample was representative of the population being observed.
The researchers proposed a hypothesis.

questions.explanation: Answer key not available yet for this imported exam.

questions.prompt:
Researchers at an advertising agency conducted a study to determine whether classical conditioning principles could influence consumers' preferences for a particular product. They randomly assigned 100 college students from a nearby university to one of two groups.

Both groups watched a commercial for the same brand of shoes. One group watched a commercial that did not feature a celebrity endorsement, while the second group watched a commercial that featured a celebrity endorsement from a physically attractive celebrity. The participants were exposed to these ads multiple times a day for two weeks.

At the end of the two weeks, the participants were asked to rate, on a scale of 1 to 10, how likely they were to purchase the brand of shoes advertised in the commercial they watched.

options.content: All 100 participants in the study were part of the experimental group.
The group that watched a commercial for the shoes that did not include a celebrity endorsement was the experimental group.
The group that watched a commercial for the shoes featuring a physically attractive celebrity was the experimental group.
This research did not involve an experimental group because there was no independent variable.

Danny has an intense fear of snakes. At the sight of a snake, he experiences a powerful fight-flight-freeze response. He avoids all places where he thinks snakes might be.

questions.prompt:
Dr. Robles conducted a study to investigate the influence of emotion on problem-solving ability. She randomly assigned 100 participants to two groups of 50.

One group was shown a series of 15 highly emotional video clips and then categorized a list of 60 words using 10 different concepts. The other group categorized the list of words using the 10 concepts without watching any video clips.

Dr. Robles calculated each participant’s score based on categorizing words under the correct concepts. The more words participants categorized correctly, the higher their score.

What is the operational definition of the dependent variable in this study?

options.content: The exposure to 15 highly emotional video clips
The group that categorized the list of words without watching any video clips
The score given for accurately categorizing words under the concepts
The 100 participants being randomly assigned to two groups of 50

questions.prompt:
Mr. Berndt is teaching his AP Psychology class about the nervous system.

Which of the following statements would best help students accurately differentiate between the primary roles of glial cells and neurons?

options.content: Glial cells release chemicals known as neurotransmitters into the synapses, while neurons provide structural support.
Glial cells provide support, insulation, and waste removal functions for neurons, while neurons create and transmit electrical impulses between cells.
Glial cells and neurons are both involved in processing sensory information, while primarily glial cells receive and process sensory input from the external environment.
Glial cells are found in both the peripheral and central nervous systems, while neurons are only found in the central nervous system.

Which of the following measurement tools is Dr. Altman using?

MOTHER: What’s his name?
ETHAN: My brother’s name is Anthony.
MOTHER: Does Anthony have a brother?
ETHAN: No.

Which of the following concepts does this conversation illustrate?

questions.prompt: Which of the following scenarios best describes a person experiencing an approach-avoidance conflict?

options.content: Jorge has been accepted by his top two choices for college, both of which offer great programs and opportunities. He is struggling to choose one before the final deadline.

Zephyr is offered admission and a scholarship to a great college. They would like to accept, but the college is 200 miles from their home and the scholarship does not include the high cost of housing.

Dayane is thirteen years old and has started to question authority, including rebelling against her parents. She has started to take risks, role-play ways of behaving, and consider her future career plans.
Mayra has always said that she would never disobey her parents. However, one night she sneaks home late after curfew. She feels discomfort about her actions and decides never to do this again.

questions.prompt:
Students enrolled in an introductory psychology course want to examine the bystander effect on their campus. The students devise a study in which one of them will pretend to fall while riding a scooter. The students choose a confederate from among the group to ride the scooter. The confederate will fall at the same location when many students are present. Then, the next day, the confederate will fall at the same location when fewer students are present. After each fall, the confederate will record whether they are helped.

Which of the following best represents the description of an independent variable in this study?

options.content: The number of people at the location when the confederate fell
The level of experience of the scooter rider
The location on the campus where the confederate fell
The students on campus who watched the confederate fall off the scooter

questions.prompt:
Abby and Jordan are doing a presentation for their AP Psychology class about the role oxytocin plays in the body.

Which of the following statements would be best to include in their description of oxytocin?

options.content: It regulates appetite by signaling fullness and by helping to control food intake.
It increases mental alertness, concentration, and focus.
It promotes parent and infant bonding.
It influences the sleep/wake cycle by managing the body’s internal clock.

questions.prompt:
For more than a year, Demyana has experienced consistently high levels of distress. She has experienced distress in her school life, work life, and relationships with her peers.

Which of the following statements is most likely true of Demyana?

options.content: She has experienced immune suppression due to the prolonged high level of distress.
She has been using emotion-focused coping to successfully relieve her distress.
She has experienced fewer symptoms of anxiety and depression.
She has experienced instinctive drift through these many challenges.

questions.prompt:
While walking home, Leo saw his girlfriend hugging someone. His heart began to race, he began to sweat, and he felt tears in his eyes beginning to form. In response to those physical reactions, Leo thought, “I am feeling sad.”

As he got closer to them, Leo recognized the person his girlfriend had been hugging as her brother. As his heart continued to pound, Leo felt happiness and he thought, “She still loves me.”

Which of the following concepts best explains Leo’s interpretation of his physical responses in this scenario?

options.content: Parasympathetic nervous system activity

questions.prompt:
Clara has test anxiety. Her teacher taps his pen while students are taking a test. After several test days, Clara experiences anxiety when she hears this tapping, even on days when there is no test.

Which of the following statements is true about this scenario?

options.content: Her fear of the test is the conditioned response (CR).
The tapping of the pen is the unconditioned stimulus (UCS).
Her fear when she hears the tapping when there is no test is the conditioned response (CR).
A test in this class is the conditioned stimulus (CS).

Use only Jim’s first name.
Debrief Jim after the research session.

questions.prompt:
A recent study aimed to investigate the relationship between attribution and high school grade point average (GPA). As part of this study, high school students were asked to complete an attribution inventory that assessed their beliefs about the factors that determine their high school GPA. These factors included whether students believed their GPA was due to their intelligence and determination or luck.

The researchers focused on students who attributed their high school GPA to luck and compared their attribution inventory score with their actual high school GPA, which is shown in the graph. Higher scores indicate a more external attribution for GPA.

Attribution Inventory Score and Grade Point Average

  • The scatterplot has 14 data points.
  • The data points are in a linear pattern trending down from left to right.
  • The data points have the following approximate (Grade Point Average comma Attribution Inventory Score) coordinates:
    • (2 comma 10)
    • (2 comma 9)
    • (2 comma 8)
    • (2 comma 7)
    • (2.3 comma 6)
    • (2.6 comma 8)
    • (2.6 comma 5)
    • (3 comma 6)
    • (3.1 comma 7)
    • (3.2 comma 8)
    • (3.7 comma 3)
    • (3.8 comma 4)
    • (3.8 comma 1)
    • (4 comma 0)
  • A line of best fit is shown:
    • The line of best fit slants down from left to right.
    • The line of best fit passes through the following approximate (Grade Point Average comma Attribution Inventory Score) coordinates:
      • (2 comma 9)
      • (3 comma 5)

Which of the following is a correct interpretation of the results given in the graph?

options.content: The students’ belief in luck and its effect on GPA regresses towards the mean, and is thus important for students to understand.
If students believe that luck makes a difference in their grades, it causes them to earn lower GPAs.
The less students believe that luck plays a significant role in their GPA, the lower their GPAs are.
The more students believe that luck plays a significant role in their GPA, the lower their GPAs are.

questions.prompt:

The results are given in the graph.

Number of Words Recalled as a Function of When Read

    • Third 20 Seconds: 18

Which of the following concepts is consistent with these findings?

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the best example of a behavior reinforced on a variable-interval schedule?

options.content: Tae sends an important text message to her boss to ask if she can miss work to attend a game. She keeps checking her phone to see if her boss has responded because she does not know how long it will take for them to respond.

Ximena responds to surveys on a website because the site offers occasional cash rewards for completing surveys. She responds to a large number of surveys on the site because she does not know how many she must complete to earn the cash rewards.
Cody sells cell phones and monthly home internet subscriptions, earning $50 for every three phones sold and $100 for every five subscriptions sold.
Alexander arrives at the train station at 7:00 a.m. every day because his train arrives at 7:05 a.m.

Dissociative disorders develop as a way for individuals to cope with trauma.
Personality traits appear to be unrelated to the development of dissociative disorders.
Individuals diagnosed with dissociative disorders have more vivid episodic memories than those not diagnosed with dissociative disorders.

Assign half of the applicants to take a math test to serve as a control group
Explain to the applicants that the test has predictive validity

questions.prompt: After accidentally touching a hot pan with his hand, Benedict immediately withdraws his hand without thinking. Which of the following processes best describes this example of a spinal reflex arc?

options.content: The sensation of heat is sent to Benedict’s brain for processing. The brain then sends a message to the peripheral nervous system, causing him to remove his hand from the hot pan.
Benedict’s peripheral nervous system immediately detects the heat, processes the response, and causes Benedict to remove his hand from the hot pan. The central nervous system is not involved.
Benedict’s peripheral and central nervous systems work independently in this situation, with the peripheral nervous system moving his hand and the central nervous system perceiving the heat.
Sensory neurons in Benedict’s peripheral nervous system detect the heat and send a rapid signal to an interneuron in the spinal cord, which then directs a motor neuron to move his hand.

Students will remember lecture information 15 minutes after the lecture ends better than students will remember that information 60 minutes after the lecture ends.
Students will remember more information if they are in the same environmental space than if they are in the same mood.

Which of the following concepts illustrates Dr. Smith’s outlook?

questions.prompt:
Dr. Hart is a cognitive neuroscientist studying how sleep deprivation affects memory consolidation. Participants are given 5 minutes to memorize 30 unrelated words. Then Dr. Hart randomly assigns the participants to one of two groups. In Group 1, participants are not allowed to sleep uninterrupted. Instead, they are awakened each time their EEG shows they are in the REM sleep phase. In Group 2, participants are allowed to sleep for up to 8 uninterrupted hours. The next day, the participants are asked to recall as many unrelated words as possible.

The mean score for Group 1 is 7 words. The mean score for Group 2 is 12 words. Dr. Hart finds that the differences are statistically significant.

Which of the following research methods is Dr. Hart using?
questions.explanation: Answer key not available yet for this imported exam.

questions.prompt:

Awakening participants each time their EEG indicates REM sleep
The number of unrelated words remembered after the observed night of sleep

questions.prompt:
Dr. Hart is a cognitive neuroscientist studying how sleep deprivation affects memory consolidation. Participants are given 5 minutes to memorize 30 unrelated words. Instead, they are awakened each time their EEG shows they are in the REM sleep phase. In Group 2, participants are allowed to sleep for up to 8 uninterrupted hours. options.content: Eight hours of uninterrupted sleep is the best way for the body to conserve resources.
Uninterrupted sleep enhances memory consolidation.
REM sleep typically occurs more frequently as the sleep cycle progresses.
Uninterrupted sleep improves overall well-being.

questions.prompt: Which of the following scenarios is an example of instinctive drift?

options.content: Rats more easily learn to pair pain in the form of shocks with bitter tastes than sweet tastes. This may result from an innate predisposition to avoid poisonous substances, which are often bitter.
In a study, chimpanzees stacked crates to grab a banana that was out of reach, demonstrating problem-solving through a sudden realization of a solution rather than through trial and error.
Several types of birds can learn to copy the calls of other types of birds and other animals such as frogs or dogs.

A dog learns to return a ball while playing fetch for the reward of praise. Eventually, the dog starts to chew and play with the ball, which is an innate tendency, instead of returning it, which is learned.

questions.prompt:
Researchers were interested in studying cultural differences in emotional expression. The researchers conducted a study in which all American and Japanese participants watched films of surgery both alone and in the presence of a researcher.

When both American and Japanese participants were alone, they showed the same facial expressions, namely disgust and fear. However, when in the presence of the researcher, the American participants continued to show their negative emotions, while the Japanese participants masked their negative emotions with a smile.

Which of the following concepts best explains this research finding?

questions.prompt:
Diedre took a yearlong psychology course in which brain structures were discussed in multiple units. At first, she had a hard time remembering the various functions of each brain structure. After learning about them and reviewing them in several different contexts, she can quickly and easily retrieve the function of each brain structure even months later.

Which of the following concepts most likely accounts for this change in memory?

options.content: Implicit priming of memory
